title: Partial Derivatives, Simplified
concept: A partial derivative measures how a function changes when you adjust only one input knob while holding all other input knobs completely still.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why is a slope of zero important in optimization?

options.text: It shows we have reached a peak (maximum) or a valley (minimum)
It means the model has run out of data
It means the inputs are all equal to zero

exercises.explain: Correct! A slope of zero flattens out at peaks and valleys. But what if we have multiple inputs to adjust?

cards.title: Tuning the shower
cards.body: A shower has two knobs: hot and cold water. To figure out how the hot knob affects the temperature, you turn only the hot knob while keeping the cold knob perfectly still.

cards.title: Isolating one variable
cards.body: In multi-input math, a partial derivative measures the rate of change for just one input variable, while treating all other variables as constants (frozen in place).

cards.title: The mixing board
cards.body: Think of a mixing board in a recording studio. To find how one slider affects the song's volume, you nudge just that one slider while leaving the other twenty sliders untouched.

cards.title: House size vs. age
cards.body: In a house price model with features size and age, the partial derivative for size tells you how much price changes per square foot, assuming the house's age stays exactly the same.

cards.title: One by one, not all at once
cards.body: You might think you have to change all variables at once to see how a model behaves. But in calculus, we isolate them one by one to understand their individual impact.

left.text:
Changing all knobs at once
Hard to isolate each effect

right.text:
Changing one knob at a time
Isolates that single input

exercises.prompt: What does a partial derivative do?

options.text: Measures how the output changes when you adjust only one input and hold others constant
Calculates only half of the total error

exercises.explain: A partial derivative isolates the effect of a single variable by freezing all other variables.

exercises.prompt: True or false: To calculate a partial derivative for variable X, we treat all other variables as if they are constant numbers.
exercises.explain: True! Treating other variables as constants makes it easy to find the slope for just variable X.

exercises.sentence: If we want to find the effect of house size on price while freezing age, we calculate the ___ derivative of price with respect to size.
exercises.explain: A partial derivative isolates the rate of change for one specific variable while freezing the rest.

exercises.prompt: Which analyst is using a partial derivative approach?

options.text: Sarah changes the advertising budget while keeping the product price constant
John changes both the advertising budget and the product price at the same time
Leo keeps all inputs exactly the same and changes nothing

exercises.explain: Sarah is isolating one input while holding others constant, which is a partial derivative approach.

exercises.prompt: A model predicts crop yield based on water and fertilizer. The partial derivative with respect to water is +3, and the partial derivative with respect to fertilizer is -2. What is the best way to increase crop yield?
exercises.explain: Fantastic! Water has a positive slope (more water = more yield), while fertilizer has a negative slope (more fertilizer = less yield). You've mastered how to isolate inputs with partial derivatives! Next up: finding the steepest way up!
